reasoning: AC.L1-3.1.1 establishes who is authorized to access the system at all. AC.L1-3.1.2 then constrains what those already-authorized users can do once inside. The two practices form a sequence: first decide if anyone gets in, then decide what they can do. The CMMC Assessment Guide explicitly notes that 3.1.2 addresses access authorizations beyond the basic account-type controls covered by 3.1.1, so the role/permission matrix work for 3.1.2 only makes sense once the user inventory for 3.1.1 is solid.

Flow control under AC.L2-3.1.3 must reference designated sources and destinations — users, devices, and external systems. AC.L1-3.1.1's identification of authorized users, processes, and devices provides the foundational entity inventory that flow rules reference. You cannot write a rule like 'only finance users may transmit CUI to the prime contractor portal' if you have not first identified who the finance users are or what counts as the prime contractor's system.

Role-based transaction limits feed directly into flow control authorizations. If AC.L1-3.1.2 establishes that only certain roles can perform certain operations, those role definitions become the basis for who may originate, receive, or forward CUI under AC.L2-3.1.3. Inconsistency between the two — where flow rules assume role boundaries that the actual RBAC does not enforce, or vice versa — is a frequent consultant finding and a common source of audit fatigue when fixing one practice forces rework in the other.

Separation of duties is enforced primarily through the role and permission structures defined under AC.L1-3.1.2. The role/permission matrix is the mechanism that prevents one individual from holding both halves of a separated duty pair. If AC.L1-3.1.2 is weak — overlapping roles, excessive group memberships — separation of duties becomes impossible to enforce technically, no matter what the SoD matrix says.

Least privilege and separation of duties are mutually reinforcing. Least privilege limits each individual to the minimum permissions they need; separation of duties ensures critical end-to-end processes require multiple individuals. When least privilege is enforced rigorously, separation of duties tends to fall out naturally — nobody ends up holding both halves of a critical pair because nobody has more permissions than they need in the first place.

AC.L2-3.1.6 (use non-privileged accounts for non-security functions) requires having identified privileged versus non-privileged accounts in the first place — which is exactly what AC.L2-3.1.5 establishes. Without the privileged account inventory and least-privilege scoping from AC.L2-3.1.5, the distinction the practice depends on doesn't exist.

AC.L2-3.1.7 prevents non-privileged users from executing privileged functions and requires logging when those functions execute. Both halves depend on the definitions established by AC.L2-3.1.5: which accounts are privileged, which functions are security functions. Without that foundation, the enforcement and logging boundaries are undefined.

The Assessment Guide notes that AC.L2-3.1.7 extends AC.L1-3.1.2 — the basic limitation of transactions and functions to authorized users is what gets refined when AC.L2-3.1.7 specifically prevents non-privileged users from executing privileged functions. The role/permission structures established for AC.L1-3.1.2 are the technical mechanism that implements both.

Account lockout under AC.L2-3.1.8 requires the system to know which authentication attempts belong to which account — and that requires the user identification work done under AC.L1-3.1.1. Lockout enforcement on shared or unidentified accounts has no meaningful target.

Session lock and session termination are complementary controls addressing the same underlying risk (unattended sessions) at different time horizons. Lock activates after short inactivity; termination after longer inactivity or other defined conditions. Implementations typically configure both in the same place (GPO, MDM) and the policies reinforce each other — lock is the first line of defense, termination is the cleanup.

Remote access control depends on knowing who is authorized to access the system at all. AC.L1-3.1.1's identification of authorized users and devices is what AC.L2-3.1.12 enforces over remote channels. Remote access without underlying access control is just a different attack surface for unauthorized users.

AC.L2-3.1.12 requires controlled remote access; AC.L2-3.1.13 requires that remote access be cryptographically protected. The Assessment Guide explicitly identifies these as complementary remote access practices. Encryption is the technical mechanism that makes controlled remote access trustworthy — without it, controlled remote access still leaks data in transit.

Routing remote access through managed access control points (AC.L2-3.1.14) is what makes the monitoring and control required by AC.L2-3.1.12 practical. Concentrating all remote access at a few chokepoints means there is somewhere to apply the controls and somewhere to capture the logs. Direct internet exposure of individual systems would make AC.L2-3.1.12 effectively unimplementable at scale.

Privileged remote access carries higher consequences if intercepted, which makes the FIPS-validated encryption from AC.L2-3.1.13 even more important here. The Assessment Guide identifies AC.L2-3.1.13 as a complementary control to AC.L2-3.1.15 — privileged remote command execution should always happen over cryptographically protected channels.

The Assessment Guide notes that AC.L2-3.1.15 extends AC.L1-3.1.2 — the basic transaction and function limits that define what privileged users can do are what AC.L2-3.1.15 then specifically authorizes (or restricts) for remote execution. Not every privileged command should be available remotely, even to users authorized to execute it locally.

Wireless authorization (3.1.16) and wireless protection (3.1.17) are complementary practices in the Assessment Guide. Authorizing a wireless connection only matters if the connection is then protected with authentication and encryption — and the protections only matter if access has been authorized. The two practices are typically configured on the same wireless controller and reviewed together.

The Assessment Guide identifies AC.L2-3.1.16, AC.L2-3.1.17, and AC.L2-3.1.18 as complementary practices controlling the connection of mobile and wireless devices through authentication, authorization, and encryption. Wireless protection (3.1.17) is the mechanism by which mobile devices (3.1.18) connect securely — most mobile devices in scope handling CUI use wireless as their primary network path.

AC.L2-3.1.19 (encrypt CUI on mobile devices) requires identifying which mobile devices handle CUI in the first place — which is exactly what AC.L2-3.1.18[a] establishes. The mobile device inventory built for connection control is the same list that drives encryption enforcement scope.

External connection control under AC.L1-3.1.20 requires identifying connections to and use of external systems — which extends the device and system identification work done under AC.L1-3.1.1. The 'devices and other systems authorized to connect' list includes external systems, so the two practices share their underlying inventory.

External connections frequently are the destinations for CUI flows — partner portals, prime contractor systems, government customer systems. AC.L1-3.1.20's identification and verification of external connections directly feeds AC.L2-3.1.3's requirement to identify designated destinations for CUI. Subcontractor connections that are ad hoc under 3.1.20 will also be ad hoc under 3.1.3.

Mobile device encryption (3.1.19) and portable storage controls (3.1.21) both protect CUI on devices that move outside the controlled environment. The same encryption requirements that apply to laptops typically apply to authorized portable storage — encrypted enterprise USB drives are the common implementation pattern that satisfies both practices.
